architecture.summary: Concept Layers (CLs) are non-trainable modules inserted between slices of a pretrained LLM. Each CL projects the model's latent representation into an interpretable conceptual space via cosine similarity to a concept set, then reconstructs and passes the representation back to the model. The suffix of the model is briefly retrained via feature-based distillation (welding) to adapt to information loss from the projection-reconstruction bottleneck, while the projection matrices remain frozen to preserve semantic meaning. Multiple CLs can be stacked at different depths for hierarchical interpretability.
trained_components: suffix model parameters (via feature-based distillation welding)

biblio.title: Concept Layers: Enhancing Interpretability and Intervenability via LLM Conceptualization

claims.statement: Concept Layers integrate interpretability and intervenability into existing pretrained models without requiring labeled concept datasets or major architectural modifications, enabling seamless integration into existing system pipelines.
Conceptualized models maintain classification performance on par with or slightly better than the original model across AG News, Yelp, and DBpedia datasets.
Task-specific Single CL on AG News achieves 91.86% accuracy vs 91.63% for original model.
Conceptualized models maintain high agreement with the original model: task-specific Double CL on AG News achieves 96.45% agreement with original.
Backward compatibility: a classifier trained on the original model transferred to task-specific Single CL on Yelp maintains 89.50% accuracy.
Automatic ontology-based concept selection via variance-guided search algorithm eliminates the need for human-selected concept sets, enabling both task-specific and task-agnostic conceptualization.

Intervenability interface enables attenuation of specific concept activations during inference to adjust model behavior dynamically, demonstrated by mitigating price-bias in Yelp Polarity classification through discounting the Economy concept.

The welding phase requires direct access to model latent representations during training or precomputing them in advance, both of which can be challenging depending on system constraints, representing the primary computational cost of the method.
